arXiv:2403.04108v1  [math.PR]  6 Mar 2024MONOTONICITY OF RECURRENCE IN RANDOM WALKS
RUPERT LI, ELCHANAN MOSSEL, AND BENJAMIN WEISS
Abstract. We consider non-homogeneous random walks on the positive qu adrant in two dimen-
sions. In the 1960‚Äôs the following question was asked: is it t rue if such a random walk Xis recurrent
andYis another random walk that at every point is more likely to go down and more likely to go
left than Y, thenYis also recurrent?
We provide an example showing that the answer is negative. We also show that if either the
random walk XorYis suÔ¨Éciently homogeneous then the answer is in fact positiv e.
1.Introduction
For the non-homogeneous reÔ¨āecting random walk on Nwith transitions pnfromnton+1 and
qn= 1‚ąípnfromnton‚ąí1 (forn >0) there is an explicit characterization of when the random
walk is recurrent/transient (see [2, Section XV.8]). From t his it follows directly that if the random
walk is recurrent and we consider a diÔ¨Äerent one in which for al lnthep‚Ä≤
nsatisfyp‚Ä≤
n‚Č§pnthen the
new random walk is also recurrent, while if the Ô¨Ārst was trans ient while the second satisÔ¨Āes p‚Ä≤
n‚Č•pn
for allnthen the second is also transient. These monotonicity prope rties can also be easily seen
via a coupling argument.
In the early 1960‚Äôs the question as to whether or not a similar monotonicity property holds for
two dimensional random walks circulated among the probabil ists at Princeton University. To the
best of our knowledge this question has not been resolved to d ate. The purpose of the following
note is to resolve this question.
We consider non-homogeneous random walks on the positive qu adrant in two dimensions. Such
a random walk is a Markov chain where the state space is N2and one may only move to one of the
four states immediately left, right, up, or down from the cur rent state. We refer readers to [4] for
a broad overview of random walks.
Our main interest is in understanding if recurrence is a mono tone property with respect to the
following partial order:
DeÔ¨Ānition 1.1. For two random walks XandYonN2, sayX‚ąĚpr‚ĆČc‚ĆČ‚Ćą‚ĆČs‚ĆČqualY, or equivalently Y‚ąĚ{ollows‚ĆČqualX, if for
each state, the probability of going down is weakly greater i nXthan inY, the probability of going
left is weakly greater in Xthan inY, the probability of going up is weakly greater in Ythan inX,
and the probability of going right is weakly greater in Ythan inX.
Intuitively, X‚ąĚpr‚ĆČc‚ĆČ‚Ćą‚ĆČs‚ĆČqualYmeansXgoes down and left more than Y.
The main question we address in the paper is the following:
Question 1.2. LetXandYbe two non-homogeneous irreducible random walks on N2withX‚ąĚpr‚ĆČc‚ĆČ‚Ćą‚ĆČs‚ĆČqualY.
Is it true that if Yis recurrent then so is X? In other words is it true that if Xis transient then
so isY?
Note that for two irreducible Markov chains XandYon the same state space, if there only
exist Ô¨Ānitely many states for which the outgoing transition probabilities are diÔ¨Äerent for XandY,
i.e.,XandYagree on a coÔ¨Ānite set of states, then they will have the same r ecurrence behavior:
Xis recurrent, positive recurrent, or transient if and only i fYis recurrent, positive recurrent,
or transient, respectively. Thus, when considering our que stion of monotonicity of recurrence, we
12 RUPERT LI, ELCHANAN MOSSEL, AND BENJAMIN WEISS
can generalize DeÔ¨Ānition 1.1, as well as all other deÔ¨Ānition s that follow in this paper, so that the
required inequalities only need to hold on a coÔ¨Ānite set of st ates. However, for sake of clarity and
brevity, we henceforth do not discuss this equivalence up to a coÔ¨Ānite set.
The intuition behind this question is that if Yis recurrent then it returns to the origin with
probability 1. As X‚ąĚpr‚ĆČc‚ĆČ‚Ćą‚ĆČs‚ĆČqualYit is perhaps natural to expect that Xwill also return to the origin with
probability 1, as indeed is the case in dimension one.
In our main result we prove that, in fact, the answer is negati ve.
Theorem 1.3. There exists two non-homogeneous random walks X‚ąĚpr‚ĆČc‚ĆČ‚Ćą‚ĆČs‚ĆČqualYsuch that Yis positive
recurrent and Xis transient. Moreover XandYare elliptic, meaning all possible transitions have
positive probabilities.
On the other hand, we do Ô¨Ānd conditions under which monotonic ity preserves recurrence.
DeÔ¨Ānition 1.4. Say a random walk on N2isinward-homogeneous if it is of the following form:

We now show that under an inward-homogeneity condition, rec urrence and positive recurrence
are monotonic properties with respect to ‚ąĚpr‚ĆČc‚ĆČ‚Ćą‚ĆČs‚ĆČqual.
Theorem 1.5. If an irreducible inward-homogeneous random walk XonN2is recurrent (respec-
tively, positive recurrent), then any irreducible random w alkYonN2such that Y‚ąĚpr‚ĆČc‚ĆČ‚Ćą‚ĆČs‚ĆČqualXis also
recurrent (respectively, positive recurrent).
For the reverse direction, where we want transience of Xto imply transience of YforY‚ąĚ{ollows‚ĆČqualX, we
canof coursetake thecontrapositive ofTheorem1.5, butthi s requires Ytobeinward-homogeneous.
The following theorem instead assumes Xis inward-homogeneous, and its proof is very similar to
that of Theorem 1.5.
Theorem 1.6. If an irreducible inward-homogeneous random walk XonN2is transient, then any
irreducible random walk YonN2such that Y‚ąĚ{ollows‚ĆČqualXis also transient.
Using Theorems 1.5 and 1.6 and their contrapositives, we obt ain the following result that allows
for application of monotonicity when neither random walk is inward-homogeneous, though an
inward-homogeneous random walk must exist between the two r andom walks.
Corollary 1.7. LetX,Y, andZbe irreducible random walks on N2whereYis inward-homogeneous
andX‚ąĚpr‚ĆČc‚ĆČ‚Ćą‚ĆČs‚ĆČqualY‚ąĚpr‚ĆČc‚ĆČ‚Ćą‚ĆČs‚ĆČqualZ. IfZis recurrent then Xis recurrent, and if Xis transient then Zis transient.
A similar monotonicity question can be asked for slabs, i.e. , sets of the form {(i,j)‚ąąZ2:i‚Č•
0,0‚Č§j‚Č§k}, which can be viewed as an interpolation between the one-dim ensional environmentMONOTONICITY OF RECURRENCE IN RANDOM WALKS 3
of non-homogeneous random walks on N, for which monotonicity always holds trivially, and the
two-dimensional environment that we study. We obtain analo gous results for slabs in Section 4.
Rayleigh‚Äôs monotonicity law, introduced by Rayleigh [3] in 1899, may be able to make some
statements on this question of when monotonicity of recurre nce occurs, but is severely limited by
the requirement that both XandYmust be Markov chains that correspond to electric networks
(see [1] for a comprehensive overview of Rayleigh‚Äôs monoton icity law). A Markov chain corresponds
to an electric network if there exist constants cxy‚Č•0 for every unordered pair of two distinct states

In Section 2, we prove Theorem 1.3. In Section 3, we prove Theo rems 1.5 and 1.6. Lastly, in
Section 4, we prove analogues of Theorems 1.3, 1.5 and 1.6 and Corollary 1.7 for slabs.
2.A Counterexample to Monotonicity
In this section, we show that recurrence, positive recurren ce, and transience are not monotonic
properties of random walks on N2with respect to the partial order ‚ąĚpr‚ĆČc‚ĆČ‚Ćą‚ĆČs‚ĆČqual. To do so, we construct
a positive recurrent random walk Xand a transient random walk Ysuch that Y‚ąĚpr‚ĆČc‚ĆČ‚Ćą‚ĆČs‚ĆČqualX, proving
Theorem 1.3.
See Figure 1 for a visual schematic of the constructions for XandY. For each random walk, the
transition probabilities for the states on the positive x-axis are the same, as well as for the states
on the positive y-axis, and for the states not on either axis; in that sense, th ere are only four types
of vertices, and we draw the expectation of their transition for each of the four types.
As one can see, the behavior on the X-axis is transient while the behavior on the Y-axis is
recurrent. For the recurrent walk in the interior of the quad rant, there is a drift to the left while
for the transient walk there is a drift downwards. This is the idea behind the following example.

4.Random Walks on a Slab
Forpositiveinteger k, lettheslabofthickness kbe{(i,j)‚ąąZ2:i‚Č•0,0‚Č§j‚Č§k}. Intuitively, the
slab is an intermediate environment for random walks betwee n the 1-dimensional random walks on
Nand the 2-dimensional random walks on N2, which are recovered in the cases k= 0 and k=‚ąě,
respectively. For a slab of thickness k, we partition the slab into six (possibly empty) regions:
thecenter{(i,j) :i‚Č•1,0< j < k }, theupper boundary {(i,k) :i‚Č•1}, thelower boundary
{(i,0) :i‚Č•1}, theleft boundary {(0,j) : 0< j < k}, the origin (0 ,0), and the upper corner (0,k).
In Section 4.1, similar to Section 2, we provide a counterexa mple to monotonicity of recurrence,
positive recurrence, and transience of random walks on the s lab with respect to the partial order ‚ąĚpr‚ĆČc‚ĆČ‚Ćą‚ĆČs‚ĆČqual
deÔ¨Āned analogously to DeÔ¨Ānition 1.1. The construction work s for all k‚Č•2, and while a diÔ¨Äerent
construction can work for k= 1, for sake of brevity we do not study the case k= 1. Furthermore, in
Section 4.2, similar to Section 3, we show that for k‚Č•2, monotonicity holds under a diÔ¨Äerent and8 RUPERT LI, ELCHANAN MOSSEL, AND BENJAMIN WEISS
arguably morenatural partial orderof randomwalks on thesl ab, assuminga homogeneity condition
similar to DeÔ¨Ānition 1.4 of inward-homogeneity. Recalling the deÔ¨Ānition of inward-homogeneity is
based on transition probabilities ‚Ąďq,rq,uq, anddqin the general case of states lying on neither axis,
this concept of homogeneity does not neatly transfer to the c asek= 1, where there is no center.
Thus, we henceforth restrict attention to k‚Č•2.
4.1.A counterexample to monotonicity. Using the essentially identical deÔ¨Ānition of ‚ąĚpr‚ĆČc‚ĆČ‚Ćą‚ĆČs‚ĆČqualfor
randomwalks on N2from DeÔ¨Ānition 1.1 forrandom walks on the slab, we Ô¨Ārstshow t hat recurrence,
positive recurrence, and transience are not monotonic prop erties of random walks on the slab with
respect to the partial order ‚ąĚpr‚ĆČc‚ĆČ‚Ćą‚ĆČs‚ĆČqual. In this sense, random walks on the slab are similar to random
walks on N2, and our construction of a positive recurrent random walk Xand a transient random
walkYsuch that Y‚ąĚpr‚ĆČc‚ĆČ‚Ćą‚ĆČs‚ĆČqualXfollows the same intuition. For our random walk on N2, the positive
x-axis moves outwards while the positive y-axis moves inwards and the primary change from Xto
Yis changing the center from predominantly moving towards th ey-axis to predominantly moving

4.2.Monotonicity in Homogeneous Random Walks. As the slab has Ô¨Ānite thickness, there
is no reason to believe that increasing the down probabiliti es will increase the chance that the
random walk is recurrent; by reÔ¨āecting across y=k/2, the up and down probabilities play identical
roles. And as seen in Section 4.1, increasing the down probab ilities can allow for counterexamples
of monotonicity. To this end, we introduce a more natural deÔ¨Ā nition for a partial order on random
walks on the slab.
DeÔ¨Ānition 4.1. For two random walks XandYon the slab of thickness k, sayX/triangleleftequalY, or
equivalently Y/trianglerightequalX, if for each state, the probability of going left is weakly gr eater inXthan in
Y, the probability of going right is weakly greater in Ythan inX, the probability of going up is
the same in Xas inY, and the probability of going down is the same in Xas inY.
Intuitively, X/triangleleftequalYmeansXgoes left more than Y. Similar to DeÔ¨Ānition 1.4, we introduce a
deÔ¨Ānition of a homogeneous random walk on the slab such that m onotonicity holds.
DeÔ¨Ānition 4.2. Say a random walk on the slab is homogeneous if it is of the following form:

Similar to an inward-homogeneous random walk on N2, we can view a homogeneous random
walk on the slab as being given by the four transition probabi lities from the general center case,
along with redirection rules if a forbidden move is selected . Within this redirection framework, the

coupling of the upper corner and boundary redirection rules such that if both attempt to move up
and the upper boundary redirects to the right, then the upper corner must redirect to the right as
well, and symmetrically for the origin and lower boundary.

Corollary 4.5. For positive integer k‚Č•2, letX,Y, andZbe irreducible random walks on the
